symbols: Lazarus rising from the tomb — the power of Christ over death, prefiguring his own Resurrection
grave cloths still on the body — the boundary between death and life being crossed
kneeling woman — Mary or Martha at Jesus's feet, faith rewarded by the miracle
onlookers' amazement — the witnesses whose belief was tested and confirmed
city and fortress behind — Bethany near Jerusalem, the world where death still reigns but is now challenged
description: A crowded scene before a medieval city with towers and a gate. At center, a figure in dark robes (Christ) stands with one hand raised toward a man emerging from a stone tomb at left. The risen man, pale and partially wrapped in white grave cloths, sits upright in the open sarcophagus. A woman in green robes kneels at Jesus's feet. A woman in white holds a cloth to her nose. Other figures — some with halos, some richly dressed — react with gestures of amazement, prayer, and discussion. The background shows a walled city, green hills, and blue mountains.
theological_description: The raising of Lazarus (John 11:1-44) is the culmination of Jesus's signs in the Fourth Gospel and the immediate provocation for his arrest and crucifixion. Jesus declares to Martha: 'I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die' (John 11:25). This is not merely a miracle but a theophanic self-revelation — Jesus claims to be the very principle of resurrection itself. Lazarus dead four days (John 11:39) demonstrates that this is no mere resuscitation but a foretaste of the eschatological resurrection. Martha's earlier complaint — 'Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died' (John 11:21) — gives way to the most powerful single command in the Gospels: 'Lazarus, come out!' (John 11:43). The woman holding a cloth to her nose references Martha's warning about the stench of decomposition (John 11:39). This miracle seals Jesus's fate — the Sanhedrin decides he must die (John 11:53) — making the giver of life the target of death.

scripture_refs.reason: The complete narrative of the raising of Lazarus from the dead
Jesus's declaration: I am the resurrection and the life
This miracle provokes the plot to kill Jesus — life given leads to life taken
God opening graves and raising the dead — Ezekiel's vision fulfilled in Christ's power
